data.title: JQC: This Fund Could Be A Very Useful Hedge If The Fed Does Not Cut (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund (JQC) is a closed-end fund that aims to provide its investors with a very high level of current income. The fund does actually manage to deliver reasonably well on this promise, as it boasts a 12.27% yield at the current share price. This is obviously much higher than the yields of just about any bond index fund, as well as most individual bonds. In fact, the current yield of a typical B-rated corporate bond issued by an American company today is about 7.41%, which is obviously well below the yield of JQC’s common shares. The fund has another advantage besides simply yield, and that is its diversification. As the fund’s portfolio consists of numerous individual debt securities, its actual exposure to any individual company is much lower than is the case for an investor who simply purchases a single high-yield debt security. This should result in investors in this fund having a lower risk of losing all of their investment to default, which is something that is always a risk when investing in high-yield debt. However, the specter of inflation is still very much present in today’s world, and as such, the real returns that investors in any type of debt security are likely to be much worse than their headline yields suggest. In addition, this fund could struggle to cover its distribution if the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates this year, but recent events make that less certain than a few months ago. As such, there are several things to consider before purchasing the shares of this fund.

The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund Versus Debt Indices

As mentioned in the introduction, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund has a 12.27% yield at the current share price. This is substantially higher than the yield of any of the major American or global bond market indices. We can see that in this comparison chart:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.93%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.68%

Vanguard Total World Bond ETF (BNDW)

4.16%

Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF (BNDX)

4.45%

J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index (EMB)

5.07%

For quite some time now, the highest-yielding of the major American, global, or international bond indices is the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, which tracks the price and yield performance of a group of junk bonds issued by American corporate entities. The J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index, which tracks the price and yield of a portfolio of U.S. dollar-denominated sovereign bonds issued by emerging market nations, has seen its yield increase fairly rapidly over the past few weeks, though. This is because the price of the index has fallen substantially since late February, when hostilities erupted between the United States, Israel, and Iran:

This chart shows the share price of the iShares J.P. Morgan USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF since the start of the year. The basic strategy of this exchange-traded index fund is to purchase all of the bonds that comprise the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index and then contract with a large financial company (the authorized participant) to create and destroy shares of the fund in order to keep the share price in line with the net asset value. Thus, the price of the exchange-traded index fund should be a pretty good proxy for the index itself. As we can see, the index really started declining in earnest on February 28, 2026, which was the same day that the United States and Israel began the first wave of airstrikes on Iran. That was the catalyst that prompted investors in most asset classes to adopt a risk-off stance and sell risky assets. Emerging market bonds (including ones issued by sovereign nations) are frequently considered to be riskier than sovereign bonds issued by developed nations, so they usually get sold off whenever investors become wary about risk. This was the case since the start of this month, and as such, investors can now obtain higher yields from emerging market bonds than was previously the case. However, the yield of the iShares J.P. Morgan USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF is still lower than the yield of American junk bond index funds as well as the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund.

However, the composition of all of the exchange-traded index funds that were just shown is somewhat different from the composition of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund. The fund’s most recent holdings report as of the time of writing is dated October 31, 2025. This document states that the fund had the following composition as of that date:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

% of Total Assets

Asset-Backed Securities

8.1%

4.9%

Common Stocks

0.9%

0.6%

Corporate Bonds

30.7%

18.7%

Variable Rate Senior Loan Interests

118.0%

71.8%

Short-Term Investments

Perhaps the biggest difference between JQC’s portfolio and the portfolios of the exchange-traded index funds just mentioned is that the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund was primarily invested in variable-rate senior loans as of the end of October 2025. The index funds just shown, however, were entirely invested in fixed-rate traditional bonds. Those assets work very differently due to the simple fact that the coupon payments made by traditional bonds do not typically change over the lifetime of the bond. A variable-rate security, on the other hand, pays a coupon that pays an amount of money that changes whenever some interest-rate benchmark changes. For example, take a look at the stated yields of some of the holdings listed in JQC’s most recent schedule of investments:

We can see that nearly all of these securities explicitly state that their coupon will change based on the yield of either the Secured Overnight Financing Rate, the one-month Term SOFR, or the three-month Term SOFR rates. These are all short-term interest rate benchmarks used in the United States, and they are very heavily influenced by the short-term interest rate policy that is set by the Federal Reserve. The yield of traditional bonds, on the other hand, is not directly controlled by the central bank but, rather, is determined by private investors’ expectations with respect to inflation, default risk, and availability of alternative uses for the same money. In addition, and perhaps most importantly, the price of variable-rate debt securities does not typically change in response to interest rate movements. This is because the coupon rate of the security changes instead. As such, it is much more difficult to profit from the price movements of these securities than it is with traditional fixed-rate bonds. Rather, the only real way to make money from these securities is to simply collect the coupon payments that they pay out. This is important because this also means that traditional fixed-rate bonds are better securities to hold during periods of time in which interest rates are declining. Variable-rate securities are generally better securities to hold whenever interest rates are rising.

The fact that the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund had most of its assets invested in variable-rate securities in late October means that it is not necessarily comparable to the traditional bond index funds shown above. While it is true that both JQC and the bond index funds may attract consideration from investors who are looking to earn an income and invest in debt securities (which are usually considered to be less risky than common stocks), the market performance and income produced by the portfolios of each of the funds will differ. As such, it might be more appropriate to compare the yield of JQC to the yield of a variable-rate exchange-traded index fund. One fund that fits this criteria is the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF (FLOT), which aims to track the price and yield performance of the Bloomberg US Floating Rate Note < 5 Years Index. This particular index fund has a 4.72% yield at the current share price, which is a bit better than some of the bond index funds, but still lower than the yield of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund.

However, even the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF might not be perfectly comparable to JQC. This comes from the fact that the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF is entirely invested in investment-grade debt securities. The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund, on the other hand, is not. The fund’s website provides the following breakdown of its portfolio as of February 28, 2026:

An investment-grade debt security is anything that is rated BBB or higher. As we can see in the chart, that description only applies to 12.8% of JQC’s portfolio. The remainder of the fund’s assets is invested in speculative-grade securities, which are colloquially known as “junk bonds.” Speculative-grade securities almost always have higher yields than investment-grade securities, a feature that is intended to compensate investors for the higher default risk of these securities. As the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund is mostly invested in speculative-grade securities, we can expect its income to he higher than the income of FLOT, all else being equal. As both closed-end funds and ETFs distribute nearly all of their investment income to their shareholders, we would expect this to give the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund a higher yield. We do, in fact, see that.

There is, fortunately, an exchange-traded index fund that tracks the price and yield performance of an index of speculative-grade variable-rate debt securities. This is the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN), which yields 7.02% at the current share price. The Invesco Senior Loan ETF tracks the Morningstar LSTA US Leveraged Loan 100 Index, which is primarily an index of junk-rated variable-rate debt. The fact sheet for the index provides the following breakdown in terms of credit quality:

We can certainly see a difference between this and the portfolio of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund. In particular, JQC has a higher allocation to BB-rated debt than the index, and it also has a higher allocation to the lowest-quality debt ratings. This will have an impact on the relative yields of each of the funds, but we can immediately see that the Invesco Senior Loan ETF is more competitive with JQC in terms of yield than any of the other exchange-traded index funds. It is also the closest to JQC in terms of composition, so that makes sense.

The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund Versus Peer Funds

As we just saw, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund has a considerably higher yield than any of the most comparable exchange-traded index funds. One reason for this appears to be that the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund is invested in somewhat riskier assets than many of the exchange-traded index funds. However, another reason is that JQC uses leverage in the management of its portfolio, whereas the index funds do not. The way that this works is that the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund borrows money and then uses that borrowed money to purchase debt securities. The securities that it purchases with this borrowed money make coupon payments to the fund, and the fund uses this income to pay the interest on its borrowings. If the coupon payments that the fund receives are higher than the interest on its own debt, then it is able to retain the surplus to boost the effective yield of the portfolio. This is usually going to be the case as the fund is buying junk debt, but should be able to borrow at investment-grade interest rates.

The most recent annual report for the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund corresponds to the full-year period that ended on July 31, 2025. For this full-year period, JQC received interest income of $107,289,805 from the debt securities that it had in its portfolio over the year. The fund’s average net assets over the same period were $819,170,296. Thus, the fund’s debt portfolio had an effective yield of 13.10% on net assets over the twelve-month period. This clearly shows that the fund has been able to boost its effective yield through the use of leverage. After all, the fund’s effective yield is in line with a portfolio that is entirely invested in CCC-rated bonds or lower:

The actual credit quality of the fund’s portfolio is better than this, though, a feat which it was able to achieve through the use of leverage.

The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund is not the only fund that is able to employ leverage as a means of boosting the yield that it earns from its portfolio. In fact, most closed-end funds employ a similar strategy to achieve that same objective. As such, we can assume that other closed-end funds that employ similar strategies to the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund are likely to be somewhat more competitive in terms of yield. This is indeed the case, as shown in this chart:

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

We can immediately see that this group of thirteen closed-end funds has current yields that are all over the place. The median fund out of the peer group is 12.29%, which is slightly higher than the current yield of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund. However, it is possible that this is somewhat misleading. This is because it is questionable whether or not all of these funds will be able to sustain their current distributions. It is very important to investigate any fund that has a significantly higher yield than its peers very closely, as the high yield could be a sign that the market doubts the fund’s ability to sustain its distribution. One thing that can cause the market to react this way is the fund distributing more money than it actually earns from its investment portfolio and destroying its net asset value in the process. Ultimately, no fund can sustain such destruction of net asset value indefinitely since it will eventually run out of assets to distribute. This is the current situation for XFLT and ACP. This chart shows the net asset value per share of these two funds over the past three years:

As we can clearly see, the XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust has suffered a net asset value decline of 29.76%, and the abrdn Income Credit Strategies Fund has suffered a net asset value decline of 21.13% over the past three years. This is a clear sign that both funds are distributing more money than their portfolios actually earn and thus depleting their own asset base in the process. The same thing is true for the Carlyle Credit Income Fund, as its own website shows that the fund’s net asset value has fallen from $7.48 per share on January 31, 2025, to $3.69 per share on February 28, 2026. Thus, the very high distribution yields of all three of those funds are somewhat misleading, as they will likely have to cut in the near future, which will result in their investors ultimately receiving lower yields on their initial investment than they expected. In addition to this, the share prices of all three of those funds have deteriorated substantially over the past three years:

Thus, while CCIF, XFLT, and ACP may all appear to have very attractive yields on the surface, the actual returns that investors will end up receiving from them are less attractive. The income is high, but at the same time, your principal will be deteriorating. In fact, even if all of the distributions that each fund paid out were reinvested into additional shares of the fund on the distribution payment date, the only one of the three that delivered a positive total return over the past three years was ACP:

This is what I mean when I say that the distribution yields of some of those funds are misleading. It is not a case where the price of the fund will stay relatively stable while it pays out the distribution. Rather, those high-yielding funds are paying out more than they earn, and thus the assets backing each share of the fund are consistently declining.

Unfortunately, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund has the same problem, although its difficulties started much more recently. This chart shows the net asset value per share of JQC over the past three years:

As we can see, the net asset value of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund was relatively stable until early last year. It only really began to decline in earnest in late February 2025, but it stopped declining around mid-April 2025. The timing of this could suggest that the fund held some assets that got caught up in the widespread market sell-off that occurred in the first few months of 2025. However, the fund managed to partially recover from that sell-off over the spring and summer months, before once again entering into a period of declining net asset value that continues to this day.

If we look at the timing of these two net asset value declines along with the composition of the fund, it becomes fairly obvious what the cause of this was. The biggest cause was the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates during the final few months of both 2024 and 2025. As we saw earlier, a significant proportion of this fund’s assets is invested in variable-rate debt with coupon payments that are adjusted based on the Secured Overnight Financing Rate. This chart shows the Secured Overnight Financing Rate over the same three-year period as shown in the chart:

This chart compares the Secured Overnight Financing Rate to the Effective Federal Funds Rate:

We can see that there is a very high correlation between the two interest rates. In fact, the correlation between the two interest rate benchmarks is so high that we can pretty much state that the interest rate decisions of the Federal Reserve largely control the SOFR rate. This is very important for understanding the performance of JQC over the past few years. In short, the Federal Reserve cut its interest rate over the September 2024 to December 2024 period and over the September 2025 to December 2025 period. As it did this, the income received by the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund declined. However, the fund’s distribution remained stable from late 2023 through early 2026 (the fund cut it earlier this month after keeping it steady since late 2023). Thus, the fund’s income was declining, but JQC did not cut its distribution. As a result, it ended up overdistributing and depleting its net asset value. Fortunately, though, this fund did not end up destroying its net asset value to the same extent as CCIF, XFLT, and ACP. That is a positive sign for investors who are looking to ensure that their assets continue to support them for as long as possible, which likely describes most retirees and other traditional income investors.

Recent Performance

The last time that we discussed the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund was on December 31, 2025. At the time, I suggested selling the fund as the Federal Reserve was widely expected to reduce interest rates in 2026. Indeed, throughout most of 2025, President Trump was frequently demanding that the Federal Reserve aggressively cut interest rates “as soon as possible.” While the president of the United States does not have direct control over the Federal Reserve, he does have the power to appoint individuals to the Board of Governors, one of whom is also the chairman of the Federal Open Market Committee, which is responsible for setting the nation’s monetary policy and short-term interest rates. This power allows the president to influence the direction of the nation’s monetary policy, as the president could (and likely will) appoint individuals who are likely to have similar views on economics and the president’s economic agenda. Thus, President Trump is likely to appoint individuals who agree with his demand for near-term interest rate cuts. As of right now, President Trump has appointed Kevin Warsh to replace Jerome Powell when the latter’s term as Federal Reserve Chairman ends in May. While Warsh was critical of quantitative easing and the Fed’s ultra-low-interest rate policy following the financial crisis of the late 2000s, Mr. Warsh has also recently stated that he believes artificial intelligence will bring sufficient changes to the economy as to warrant near-term interest rate cuts. As we have already seen, if the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates, the income that JQC receives from its portfolio will decline, and this will ultimately force it to reduce its distribution.

However, there have been a few events that have occurred since my previous article on the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund that weaken the case for near-term interest rate cuts. One of the biggest of these changes is that inflation has started to move in the wrong direction. The Federal Reserve generally considers the Personal Consumption Expenditures Index to be a better measure of inflation than the more widely reported Consumer Price Index. According to Joseph G. Haubrich, economist emeritus for the Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland:

The Federal Reserve, however, states its goal for inflation in terms of the PCE.

He goes on to state:

Over the short term, the core measure may give a more accurate reading of where inflation is headed.

Thus, the Federal Reserve may look most closely at the Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Index when it evaluates its success at controlling inflation. This chart shows the year-over-year change in the Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Index for every month in the past year:

The most recent Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Index figure shows a 3.10% rate of inflation. Furthermore, this index states that the rate of inflation was 2.8% in November and 3% in December 2025. Thus, inflation as measured by the Federal Reserve’s preferred gauge has been getting worse, and it is more than 50% above the so-called 2% target. This alone would weaken the case for near-term interest rate cuts.

However, there is another measurement that should not be ignored when talking about inflation. This is the Producer Price Index, which measures the average change over time in the selling prices received by domestic producers of products. This chart shows the month-over-month rate of change in the Producer Price Index for each of the past twelve months:

While headlines and even economists frequently focus on the year-over-year inflation measurement figures rather than the month-over-month figures, the month-over-month figures are still very important because they provide a much more real-time picture of how prices are changing. With this in mind, look at the trend since October. The month-over-month change in the Producer Price Index was 0.1% in October 2025, and it has since increased to 0.7% in February. That is an annualized rate of change of 8.4%, obviously far above the Federal Reserve’s so-called 2% inflation target. It is unlikely that commercial producers and businesses will eat cost increases of this level indefinitely, so at some point, this will make its way down into consumer prices. As such, this trend weakens the case for near-term interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve, as it points to a worsening inflation outlook that the central bank will likely want to contain.

It is important to note that the just-described worsening inflation outlook was before another event occurred that will likely make inflation much worse in the near term. This was, of course, the outbreak of hostilities between the United States, Israel, and Iran that resulted in passage through the Strait of Hormuz becoming disrupted and a rapid increase in the price of crude oil. Since the start of March 2026, Brent crude oil has risen by 38.77%, and West Texas Intermediate crude oil has risen by 32.57%. Crude oil is an input into most products due to the simple fact that nearly everything that is physical needs to be transported in some way. In addition, many machines run on refined crude oil, and even most service employees have to pay for gasoline to travel from place to place. As such, we have a situation in which inflation was getting worse, and then crude oil prices spiked. This strongly suggests that inflation will continue to get worse before it gets better. This could make it difficult for the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates for a while.

The market seems to have caught on to this, and recent trends suggest that it agrees with this thesis. Right now, the market strongly believes that there will be either no rate cuts at all or a rate hike in 2026:

As of right now, the Federal Reserve’s current federal funds target rate is 350 to 375 basis points. Thus, the market currently thinks that there is a 54.2% chance that the Federal Reserve keeps its benchmark interest rate unchanged for the remainder of this year. There is only a 1.3% chance of a single 25-basis point cut. There is currently nobody expecting more than a single interest rate cut. The market also believes that there is a 44.7% chance of between one and four 25-basis point interest rate hikes this year.

If the market is correct, this completely changes the thesis for variable-rate securities since the last time that we discussed the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund. If the Federal Reserve were to increase interest rates, then it would increase the amount of money that this fund receives from the variable-rate assets in its portfolio. That could potentially point to a distribution increase in the future once the rate hikes increase the fund’s income sufficiently. This also suggests that variable-rate securities will outperform traditional fixed-rate bonds for the near term. Thus, if the market is correct here, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund should be upgraded from a “Sell” to a “Buy.”

Despite this, the fund’s performance since the last time that we discussed it has been rather disappointing. As we can see here, the fund’s share price has declined 3.59% since our last discussion. This is a bit worse than the price performance of several different debt indices:

While this is disappointing, it is also not unexpected. As we discussed earlier, the Invesco Senior Loan ETF is perhaps the closest of the group to JQC in terms of composition. That particular fund has declined by 2.38% since the last time that we looked at JQC. The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund employs leverage, whereas BKLN does not. One of the disadvantages of leverage is that it amplifies downward movements in asset prices. Thus, we would expect this fund’s price to decline more than BKLN during any period in which asset prices are falling.

Fortunately, though, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund did manage to do somewhat better over the period in terms of total return:

As we can see, if we assume reinvestment of all of the distributions paid out by all of the funds, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund provided its investors with a 0.49% total loss. This is still a loss, but it is still much better than many other things performed during the same period. After all, the S&P 500 Index (SP500) has declined by 3.70% over the same period. Investors who reinvested their distributions into additional shares of JQC have at least managed to come fairly close to keeping the value of their investment stable in the face of the market carnage.

Valuation

Shares of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund are currently trading at a 12.27% discount on net asset value. This is a much more attractive discount than the 9.61% discount that the fund has had on average over the past month.

The current price of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund is also very attractive compared to the fund’s historical pricing. Over the past five years, shares of JQC have traded at prices ranging from a 0.34% discount to a 15.85% discount on net asset value. The average price over the five-year period has been an 8.25% discount on net asset value. Obviously, the current price represents a larger discount than the fund’s five-year average, and thus it looks reasonably attractive as an entry point.

Here is how the current price of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund compares with that of its peers:

As with their yields, the valuations of these various funds are all over the place. The median price of the thirteen-fund group is a 12.68% discount, which is obviously very slightly better than the current price of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund. However, some of these funds are not really worth purchasing at all due to the high level of net asset value destruction. Most of the funds that are overdistributing are the ones that have very high discounts to net asset value, and if we exclude those from consideration, then the current price of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund looks reasonably attractive to the rest of its peer group. When this is combined with the fund’s large discount compared to its own historical valuation, the current price looks quite reasonable for any investor looking to buy shares of it as a hedge against rising interest rates.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund is a closed-end fund that has the bulk of its assets in variable-rate corporate debt. While that was not an especially attractive composition a few months ago, the thesis for holding variable-rate debt has improved significantly. This is because both inflation data and the recent oil price shock have weakened the case for near-term interest rate cuts and, in fact, may have made a near-term interest rate hike a realistic possibility. Variable-rate securities are among the few asset classes that benefit from rising interest rates, and as such, this fund could help offset losses in assets such as stocks or bonds that typically decline whenever interest rates rise. As such, it is appropriate to upgrade this fund to a “buy” for now. The Federal Reserve still seems committed to cutting interest rates this year (based on the most recent dot plot), so I would not recommend taking a huge position in this fund, but it could be helpful to own it as a way to ride through the current environment and earn some income along the way.

data.title: Coal's Comeback And Four Stocks To Focus On
data.description:
Introduction

Once again, and this time more severely, global oil and gas flows have been disrupted, and coal may come back to save the day. With around 17% of global LNG production trapped until the war is over and 3% offline for a few years due to infrastructure damage, Japanese, Korean, and European electricity plants will need to switch to coal to reduce costs or, in some cases, avoid blackouts. Thermal coal prices have risen from depressed levels and should provide the sector with higher margins and earnings for a few years, perhaps longer, as the world attempts to reduce conflict-zone risk in its energy supplies and ultimately moves toward clean nuclear energy and more renewables.

Coal Price Parity

As LNG supplies decrease and prices rise, power plants that use it to generate electricity may first switch to coal to reduce costs, which requires regulatory approval. Under this scenario, coal should trade at a discount to LNG parity. Today, with LNG prices around $20/MTU, high-grade thermal coal’s parity is around $152 on a caloric value basis. Many US coal companies have lower quality grades and compete with cheap natural gas. Australia generally has far higher grades and is closer to the Japanese/Korean markets, with lower transport costs. Europe may also switch to coal, but Poland has sufficient supplies that could meet demand. Nonetheless, if LNG is in deficit, then coal is the most likely to replace it in electricity generation, and the parity price may be reached.

Energy Price Spikes

The Iran war and subsequent infrastructure damage and transport closure (Hormuz) drove oil and LNG prices up by over 80%, but coal is still up around 20%. The Ukrainian invasion led to a far greater spike in LNG and coal prices. The ratio of Coal to LNG, which is normally below 10x, reached 25x during the Ukrainian impact and is now approaching 15x. There seems to be room for higher LNG and coal prices in the long term, as this conflict lasts longer, with consequences felt for many years. The market may add conflict premiums to Gulf LNG, which can push coal prices higher until new fuel and energy sources replace it.

Coal Company Comps

I gathered consensus data from a wide range of coal miners worldwide. I assume most forecasts do not yet reflect higher coal prices, but they are starting to trickle in. The sector has been largely forgotten, given the move to clean energy, and as demand fell, so have earnings for most companies. The market has, however, begun to price in better results, with price targets in negative territory driven by the Iran war. While some companies seem to have exceptional growth forecasts for 2026, most are due to a difficult 2025, when oversupply in Indonesia drove many companies into losses. Thus, the growth rates are from depressed levels. Core Natural Resources (CNR) has high growth due to M&A and recovery. Valuations are low, as expected for a mature and declining sector, with most stocks trading under 6x EV/EBITDA. On the positive side, the sector has a solid balance sheet with net cash positions.

Sensitivity Analysis

To better gauge the upside potential of higher coal prices for the sector, I conducted a sensitivity analysis for Yancoal Australia (YACAF), Whitehaven Coal (WHITF), and two US companies, CNR and Peabody (BTU). Using the current consensus forecast, I created three price scenarios. Since each company has different coal grades and geographic variations, I assumed a price increase relative to their current forecasts. In my view, 25% is a conservative estimate for the next year or two. A 50% increase may require further delays in ending the conflict, and a 75% increase would put LNG production in deficit for two years or more.

I maintained current volumes and cost structure, so the high prices go to margins and EBITDA. I then valued the four stocks at 3x EV/EBITDA without adjusting for higher cash flow. I think this is a conservative valuation approach. The results, as shown in the tables below, could drive shares higher by more than 50% in the most conservative case. A long war and/or sustained LNG disruption could lead to shares more than doubling from here.

Risk

There are two key risks to coal price increases: that the war ends soon and that LNG supplies normalize quickly. A secondary risk is regulation, the ability to switch power plants to polluting coal. Finally, there is a transport risk: can the interested parties secure bulk ships at reasonable rates to meet delivery demand?

Conclusion

Given LNG capacity curtailment and destruction, it is highly likely that many nations dependent on this fuel to produce electricity will be forced to substitute it for coal, at first due to price pressures on inflation, and then due to shortages that may provoke blackouts. Coal is the only readily available and economically viable substitute for LNG. Prices should continue to rise, and the coal companies can generate windfall profits that will drive share valuation and increase capital returns.
